text: By the end of this section, you'll be able to use the 5Y method to find system-level root causes behind sanitation failures, and you'll understand what good coaching looks like. When a pre-op fails, or an ATP result comes back hot, the natural reaction is to ask who's responsible. But that's the wrong starting point. The question isn't who. It's why. Not the surface level Y, but the deep system-level Y that reveals whether this is a one-off mistake or something fundamentally broken. The 5Y method is simple and effective. Start with the observed problem and ask why. Then ask again. By the fourth or fifth Y, you've usually reached the root cause, which is almost always a system issue, not an individual failure. Let me walk through a practical example. The mixer failed pre-op. Why? Doe residue behind the agitator seal. Why was there residue? The seal wasn't removed during cleaning. Why not? The SSOP doesn't include seal removal. Why not? Because the procedure was written for the old mixer, which had a different seal design. The new mixer was installed six months ago, but nobody updated the SSOP. That's your root cause, not the cleaner's fault. The cleaner followed the procedure. The procedure was wrong because management of change didn't trigger an SSOP review. Now fix the system. Update the SSOP. Review all SSOPs for recently changed equipment. Establish a trigger that automatically flags SSOP review whenever equipment is replaced. Nobody gets blamed. The system gets improved. Another example. ATP results on a conveyor belt are trending upward over three weeks, not failing, but consistently higher. The team follows the SSOP. Chemistry checks out. On inspection, you notice micro abrasions on the belt surface from normal wear. These trap organic material that a flat brush can't reach. The belt runs fine mechanically, but it's no longer cleanable to standard. Root cause. Preventive maintenance doesn't include hygiene criteria for belt replacement. Fix. Add belt surface condition to periodic equipment inspection and establish hygiene-based replacement thresholds. The five why works because it forces you past the obvious. The obvious answer is it wasn't cleaned properly, which leads to retraining one person. The five why answer leads to fixing the procedure, the change management process, and potentially training for all similar equipment. One fixes a symptom, the other fixes the system. Now coaching. A root cause program only works if the people doing the cleaning understand why they're doing what they're doing. Your goal is a team that doesn't just follow checklists. When someone can explain what they cleaned, how they verified it, what chemical they used and why, and what they do if verification showed a failure, your program is alive. If they say, I just do what the sheet says, it's paperwork. It works in normal conditions, but falls apart when something changes. Coaching happens on the floor, not in a classroom. Walk with your crew during cleaning, ask questions. Why do you clean this surface before that one? What happens if this chemical gets on a food surface without rinsing? Why do we remove this gasket instead of cleaning around it? When someone gives the wrong answer, explain the connection. If you don't remove this gasket, dough residue accumulates. That residue can harbor salmonella. If it falls onto the slicer belt during pre-op, that's post-bake contamination. One gasket, one step missed. That's how it connects. When someone gives the right answer, recognize it. Reinforcement matters as much as correction. A team member who can articulate the why will make the right decision when the checklist doesn't cover the situation. Recap. Use 5Y to find system-level root causes. Fix the system, not the symptom. Almost every recurring failure traces to a procedure gap, a change management failure, or a training deficiency. For coaching, your measure of success is whether your team can explain what they clean, how they verify, and what happens when it fails. If they can do that, your program is alive.
